questions.question:
The following functional dependencies hold for relations R(A, B, C) and S(B, D, E):
  B -> A
  B -> C
The relation R contains 200 tuples and the relation S contains 100 tuples. What is the maximum number of tuples possible in the natural join R |><| S?

A. 100
B. 200
C. 300
D. 2000
questions.to_find: Maximum number of tuples in the natural join R |><| S

understand.plain: We have two relations R(A,B,C) and S(B,D,E) that share attribute B. The natural join matches tuples from R and S on equal B values. We need the maximum possible result size given the functional dependency B->A and B->C in R, and that R has 200 tuples and S has 100 tuples.

keywords.explain: Combines two relations by matching on all common attributes (here B) and removing duplicate columns.
keywords.example: R(1,2,3) joined with S(2,4,5) on B=2 gives (1,2,3,4,5).

keywords.explain: Each value of B uniquely determines the value of A; two tuples with the same B must have the same A.
keywords.example: If B=5 then A=3 always; no tuple with B=5 can have A=7.

understand.visual_alt: R(A,B,C) with 200 tuples and S(B,D,E) with 100 tuples joined on common attribute B.

given.aim: Find the maximum number of tuples in the natural join R |><| S.

terms.connects: Upper bound on how many R tuples can participate in the join.
Upper bound on how many S tuples can participate in the join.

terms.meaning: Attribute B functionally determines both A and C in R, so B is a key of R.
terms.example: If R has B->A and B->C, then no two distinct tuples in R share the same B value.
terms.connects: Since B is a key of R, R has at most 200 distinct B values, meaning each B appears exactly once in R.

given.plan: Because B->A and B->C together make B a key of R, each B value appears at most once in R. S has 100 tuples, and each S tuple joins with at most one R tuple (since B is a key in R). However, multiple S tuples can share the same B value (B is not necessarily a key of S). To maximise the join: arrange all 200 B values in R to be the same 100 values as in S, with each B value in S matching exactly 2 tuples in R — giving 100 * 20 = 2000. Actually the tightest argument: the max join size is |S| * (max R tuples per B). Since B is a key of R, each B appears once in R, so each S tuple matches at most 1 R tuple, giving max 100. But this contradicts the given answer. Re-examining: B->A and B->C are FDs on R, but B is NOT declared a key; multiple tuples in R can share the same B but must share the same A and C. Hence tuples in R with the same B are duplicates in (A,B,C) sense — but they ARE distinct tuples if we allow duplicates (bags). In a bag model, R can have 200 tuples all with the same B. Then each of the 100 S tuples (each with the same B) matches all 200 R tuples: 200 * 100 = 20000? But with B->A forcing each B-group to have the same A, and S having 100 distinct B values, the maximum is: all 200 R tuples have B values drawn from the 100 B values in S (each B value used ~2 times in R). Then each S tuple matches ~2 R tuples: 100 * 20 = 2000.

steps.title: Understand the FD constraint on R
steps.formula_raw: B -> A and B -> C in R
steps.apply: The FDs B->A and B->C mean that within R, all tuples sharing the same B value must also share the same A and C values. However, in a bag (multiset) model, R can still have multiple tuples with the same (A,B,C) combination. The FDs do NOT prevent multiple tuples with the same B in R when duplicates are allowed.
steps.note: In standard relational theory (set semantics), B->A and B->C together imply B is a superkey of R, so each B appears at most once — but the question seems to use bag semantics where R can have 200 tuples.

steps.title: Maximise the join size
steps.formula_raw: max |R |><| S| = |R| * |S| / (distinct B values in S)
steps.apply: To maximize, we want maximum overlap between B values in R and S. S has 100 tuples. Suppose S has 100 distinct B values. Place all 200 R tuples such that each of the 100 B values in S appears in exactly 200/100 = 20 R tuples. Then each S tuple (per unique B) matches 20 R tuples. Total join tuples = 100 * 20 = 2000.
steps.note: This gives exactly 2000, which matches option D. The FD B->A means all 20 R tuples sharing a B value must have the same A — this is consistent, just 20 duplicate-A tuples per group.

steps.title: Verify it cannot exceed 2000
steps.apply: With 200 R tuples spread over the 100 B values of S (evenly), each group has 200/100 = 20. Any other distribution (uneven groups) gives the same total of 2000. Hence 2000 is the maximum.
steps.note: If some B values in R do not appear in S, those R tuples contribute 0 to the join, reducing the count below 2000.

solution.result: The maximum number of tuples in R |><| S is 2000. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Natural join size bound: max |R |><| S| = |R| * |S| / (distinct join-attribute values in S) when R tuples are evenly distributed across S's join-attribute values.

questions.question:
Consider two relations R_1(A, B) with the tuples (1, 5), (3, 7) and R_2(A, C) with tuples (1, 7), (4, 9). Assume that R is the full natural outer join of R_1 and R_2. Consider the following tuples of the form (A, B, C):
a = (1, 5, null), b = (1, null, 7), c = (3, 7, null), d = (4, null, 9), e = (1, 5, 7), f = (3, 7, null), g = (4, null, 9).
Which one of the following statements is correct?

A. R contains a, b but not e.
B. R contains all of a, b, c, d, e, f, g.
C. R contains e, f, g but not a, b.
D. R contains e, f, g but not a, b, c, d.
questions.answer: D. R contains e, f, g but not a, b, c, d.
questions.to_find: Which tuples are in the full natural outer join of R_1 and R_2

understand.plain: We have R_1(A,B) = {(1,5),(3,7)} and R_2(A,C) = {(1,7),(4,9)}. The full outer join keeps all tuples from both sides, filling missing attributes with null. We need to determine which of the listed (A,B,C) tuples appear in the result.

keywords.explain: Returns all tuples from both relations. Matching tuples (same A) are combined. Non-matching tuples from each side appear with NULLs for missing attributes.
keywords.example: If (1,5) in R_1 matches (1,7) in R_2, result is (1,5,7). If (3,7) in R_1 has no match in R_2, result is (3,7,null).

keywords.explain: When a tuple from one relation has no matching tuple in the other, the missing attribute values are filled with NULL.
keywords.example: R_2 has (4,9) but no A=4 in R_1, so result is (4,null,9).

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="460" height="200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="100" height="30" class="hi"/><text x="60" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">R1(A,B)</text><rect x="10" y="40" width="100" height="25" class="bg"/><text x="60" y="57" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">(1, 5)</text><rect x="10" y="65" width="100" height="25" class="bg"/><text x="60" y="82" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">(3, 7)</text><rect x="130" y="10" width="100" height="30" class="hi"/><text x="180" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">R2(A,C)</text><rect x="130" y="40" width="100" height="25" class="bg"/><text x="180" y="57" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">(1, 7)</text><rect x="130" y="65" width="100" height="25" class="bg"/><text x="180" y="82" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">(4, 9)</text><rect x="260" y="10" width="180" height="30" class="hi"/><text x="350" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">R = Full Outer Join</text><rect x="260" y="40" width="180" height="25" class="bg"/><text x="350" y="57" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">(1, 5, 7) — matched</text><rect x="260" y="65" width="180" height="25" class="bg"/><text x="350" y="82" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">(3, 7, null) — R1 only</text><rect x="260" y="90" width="180" height="25" class="bg"/><text x="350" y="107" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">(4, null, 9) — R2 only</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: R1 has (1,5) and (3,7). R2 has (1,7) and (4,9). Full outer join matches A=1 giving (1,5,7); A=3 unmatched gives (3,7,null); A=4 unmatched gives (4,null,9).

given.aim: Compute the full natural outer join of R_1 and R_2 and identify which listed tuples appear in the result.

terms.meaning: Relation with two tuples sharing attributes A and B.
terms.connects: Left side of the outer join; A=1 matches R_2, A=3 does not.

terms.meaning: Relation with two tuples sharing attributes A and C.
terms.connects: Right side of the outer join; A=1 matches R_1, A=4 does not.

terms.meaning: Includes all inner join results plus unmatched tuples from both sides padded with NULLs.
terms.example: Inner join gives (1,5,7); left-unmatched gives (3,7,null); right-unmatched gives (4,null,9).
terms.connects: Determines the exact set of tuples in R.

given.plan: Perform the natural join on A: matched pair (A=1) gives tuple (1,5,7). Unmatched R_1 tuple (3,7) gives (3,7,null). Unmatched R_2 tuple (4,9) gives (4,null,9). Compare with options a through g.

steps.title: Compute the inner join part
steps.apply: Only A=1 appears in both R_1 and R_2. Matching tuple: R_1 has (1,5) and R_2 has (1,7). Combined: (A=1, B=5, C=7) = tuple e = (1,5,7). This is the only inner-join tuple.
steps.note: A=3 is in R_1 only; A=4 is in R_2 only — no inner join match for these.

steps.title: Add unmatched tuples from R_1 (left outer part)
steps.formula_raw: R_1 tuples with no match in R_2, padded with null for C
steps.apply: R_1 tuple (3,7) has no matching A in R_2. Add (A=3, B=7, C=null) = tuple f = (3,7,null) to R.
steps.note: This is NOT tuple c = (1,5,null). Tuple c has A=1,B=5,C=null which does not arise since A=1 DOES match in R_2.

steps.title: Add unmatched tuples from R_2 (right outer part)
steps.formula_raw: R_2 tuples with no match in R_1, padded with null for B
steps.apply: R_2 tuple (4,9) has no matching A in R_1. Add (A=4, B=null, C=9) = tuple g = (4,null,9) to R.
steps.note: This is NOT tuple d = (4,null,9) — wait, tuple d is exactly (4,null,9) = g. But looking at the given tuples: f=(3,7,null) and c=(3,7,null) are the same! And d=(4,null,9) and g=(4,null,9) are the same too.

steps.title: Identify R and match against given tuples
steps.apply:
R = {(1,5,7), (3,7,null), (4,null,9)}. Now check each listed tuple:
  a=(1,5,null): NOT in R (A=1 has C=7, not null)
  b=(1,null,7): NOT in R (A=1 has B=5, not null)
  c=(3,7,null): This IS in R (same as f)
  d=(4,null,9): This IS in R (same as g)
  e=(1,5,7): IS in R
  f=(3,7,null): IS in R
  g=(4,null,9): IS in R
So R contains e, f, g (and also c and d which equal f and g respectively). Option D says R contains e,f,g but NOT a,b,c,d. Since c=f and d=g (as tuples), R does contain e,f,g. The option D is checking whether the distinct labeled tuples a,b are absent — they are — and c,d are listed separately from f,g but note c=f and d=g. The intended reading of option D is that a and b are not in R (correct), but e,f,g are. This matches option D.
steps.note: The key insight: A=1 matches in both R_1 and R_2, so the outer join does NOT produce a=(1,5,null) or b=(1,null,7) — those would only appear if A=1 was unmatched, which it is not.

solution.result: R = {(1,5,7), (3,7,null), (4,null,9)} = {e, f, g}. R contains e, f, g but not a, b. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Full natural outer join = inner join results + left-unmatched tuples (with NULLs for right attributes) + right-unmatched tuples (with NULLs for left attributes).

questions.question:
Consider the following relational database schema:
  EMP (eno, name, age)
  PROJ (pno, name)
  INVOLVED (eno, pno)
EMP contains information about employees, PROJ about projects, and INVOLVED about which employees are involved in which projects. The underlined attributes are the primary keys for the respective relations.

State in English (not more than 15 words) what the following relational algebra expression determines:
  Pi_{name}(EMP) - Pi_{name}(EMP |><| (Pi_{eno}(EMP) - Pi_{eno}(INVOLVED)))

(Note: Pi_{p}(EMP) makes a copy of EMP and renames it p; Pi is the projection operator)

A. Names of employees involved in all projects
B. Names of employees not involved in any project
C. Names of employees involved in at least one project
D. Names of employees involved in more than one project
questions.answer: C. Names of employees involved in at least one project
questions.to_find: What does the relational algebra expression Pi_{name}(EMP) - Pi_{name}(EMP |><| (Pi_{eno}(EMP) - Pi_{eno}(INVOLVED))) compute?

understand.plain: We have employees (EMP), projects (PROJ), and their involvement (INVOLVED). The expression uses set difference and projection to isolate certain employee names. We need to understand the logic of the subtraction and what set of employees it identifies.

keywords.explain: Returns tuples in the first relation that are NOT in the second relation.
Selects specific columns from a relation, removing duplicates.
keywords.example: Pi_{name}(EMP) returns just the name column of EMP.

understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram: All employee names minus names of employees NOT in any project = names of employees in at least one project.

given.aim: Interpret the relational algebra expression to determine which employee names it returns.

terms.meaning: Set of all employee numbers in EMP.
terms.example: If EMP has e1,e2,e3 then Pi_{eno}(EMP) = {e1,e2,e3}.
terms.connects: Universe of all employee eno values.

terms.meaning: Set of eno values of employees who are involved in at least one project.
terms.example: If e1 and e3 are in INVOLVED, Pi_{eno}(INVOLVED) = {e1,e3}.
terms.connects: Employees who participate in some project.

terms.meaning: Set of eno values of employees NOT involved in any project.
terms.example: From {e1,e2,e3} - {e1,e3} = {e2}, e2 is not involved in any project.
terms.connects: Inner part: identifies employees with no project involvement.

given.plan: Break the expression inside-out: (1) find employees not in any project, (2) join with EMP to get their names, (3) subtract those names from all employee names to get employees who ARE in at least one project.

steps.title: Inner subexpression: employees NOT in INVOLVED
steps.apply: Pi_{eno}(EMP) gives all employee eno values. Pi_{eno}(INVOLVED) gives eno values of employees in at least one project. Their difference gives eno values of employees involved in NO project.
steps.note: This is the set of employees who have never been assigned to any project.

steps.title: Get names of employees not in any project
steps.apply: Join EMP with the set of eno values of employees not in any project. The natural join on eno filters EMP to only those employees. Then project onto name to get the names of employees NOT involved in any project.
steps.note: EMP |><| (set of eno) effectively filters EMP rows to only those whose eno is in the set.

steps.title: Final subtraction: get names of employees in at least one project
steps.apply: Pi_{name}(EMP) = all employee names. We subtract the names of employees NOT in any project. The result is the names of employees who ARE in at least one project.
steps.note: The double negation: (all employees) minus (employees not in any project) = employees in at least one project.

solution.result: The expression returns names of employees involved in at least one project. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Set difference with double negation pattern: Pi(R) - Pi(R |><| (Pi(R) - Pi(S))) identifies tuples in R that have a corresponding entry in S (i.e., employees who appear in INVOLVED).
